steps.text: Hi there! I'm Remy the Fox, and today we're going to learn about adding numbers! Adding means putting things together to find out how many there are in all.
Let's try adding 3 and 4. Imagine you have 3 apples and someone gives you 4 more apples. How many do you have now?
Count with me! Start at 3 and count 4 more: 4, 5, 6, 7. So 3 plus 4 equals 7! You're doing amazing!
Great work! You just learned how to add numbers to 10. Let's practice together!
Excellent! Now that you can add to 10, let's try adding bigger numbers — all the way up to 20! You're going to be a superstar at this.
Let's add 8 and 7. We can start at 8 and count on 7 more. Ready? Here we go!
Count with me from 8: 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15. That's 7 steps, so 8 plus 7 equals 15!
Wonderful! You can add numbers up to 20. Let's practice some problems to make you even better!
Wow, you're getting so good! Now let's try something fun — adding THREE numbers together at the same time!
Here's a tricky one: 2 plus 3 plus 4. We can add the first two numbers first. 2 plus 3 equals 5.
Now take that 5 and add 4 more. 5 plus 4 equals 9! So 2 plus 3 plus 4 equals 9. You did it!
Amazing! You can add three numbers together. Let's practice and show off your skills!
Welcome back! Now we're going to add bigger numbers — up to 100! This is going to be so fun. Let's start with numbers that don't need regrouping.
Let's add 34 and 23. We add the ones place first: 4 plus 3 equals 7.
Then we add the tens place: 3 tens plus 2 tens equals 5 tens, which is 50. Put them together: 57!
Fantastic! Adding big numbers works the same way — just add each place value. Let's practice!
Great job! Now let's try adding when we need to regroup — that means carrying a number to the next place. You've got this!
Let's add 47 and 35. Start with the ones: 7 plus 5 equals 12. That's more than 9, so we write 2 and carry 1 ten.
Now the tens: 4 tens plus 3 tens plus the 1 we carried equals 8 tens. So 47 plus 35 equals 82!
Superstar! You just learned regrouping. It might seem tricky but you're doing brilliantly. Practice time!

lessons.title: Adding to Find Totals in Arrays

steps.text: Here's something really cool! We can use addition to count objects arranged in rows and columns — we call that an array!
Imagine 3 rows of stickers with 4 stickers in each row. We can add: 4 plus 4 plus 4. That's adding the same number three times!
4 plus 4 equals 8, then 8 plus 4 equals 12. So 3 rows of 4 stickers gives us 12 stickers in all!
Brilliant! Adding equal groups is a powerful math skill. Let's practice finding totals in arrays!
You're in third grade now — let's add really big numbers! We'll use place value to break numbers into hundreds, tens, and ones.
Let's add 300 and 400. Think of it as 3 hundreds plus 4 hundreds. 3 plus 4 equals 7, so that's 7 hundreds!
300 plus 400 equals 700. Place value makes big addition easy — you're amazing!
Perfect! Now let's practice adding hundreds together. You're going to ace this!
Ready to add numbers up to one thousand? This is where your math skills really shine! Let's add a three-digit number.
Let's add 245 and 138. Start with the ones: 5 plus 8 equals 13. Write 3, carry 1.
Tens: 4 plus 3 plus 1 carried equals 8. Hundreds: 2 plus 1 equals 3. So 245 plus 138 equals 383!
Outstanding! Adding within 1000 uses the same place-value steps. Let's practice and build your speed!
Wow, you're a math champion! Now let's try problems with two steps — where you need to add twice to get to the answer.
A school library has 350 books. They get 120 new books in the morning, and 85 more in the afternoon. How many books are there now?
Step one: 350 plus 120 equals 470. Step two: 470 plus 85 equals 555. The library has 555 books!
Then 470 + 85 = 555. Two steps done — you solved it! You're an incredible mathematician!
Now let's learn about taking away — that's called subtraction! When we subtract, we start with a number and take some away.
Imagine you have 7 grapes and you eat 3 of them. How many are left? That's 7 take away 3.
Count back from 7: 6, 5, 4. That's 3 steps back. So 7 minus 3 equals 4! You have 4 grapes left.
Wonderful! Subtraction is just counting back. Let's practice and you'll be a subtraction star!
Excellent subtraction skills! Now let's subtract with bigger numbers — up to 20. You're ready for this challenge!
Here's a problem: 15 minus 6. Start at 15 and count back 6 steps.
15... count back: 14, 13, 12, 11, 10, 9. Six steps back lands on 9. So 15 minus 6 equals 9!
You're brilliant! Counting back works for bigger numbers too. Let's do some practice problems!
Here's a cool math trick! Subtraction and addition are related — they're like best friends. We can turn a subtraction problem into a missing number problem!
If we have 10 minus 3, we can think: 3 plus what number equals 10? That unknown number is what we're looking for!
Think: 3 plus 7 equals 10. So 10 minus 3 equals 7! Addition helps us with subtraction. That's so clever!
Amazing thinking! Seeing subtraction as a missing addend makes it so much easier. Let's practice!
Time to subtract bigger numbers! Let's start with subtraction within 100 when we don't need to regroup. You can do this!
Let's try 68 minus 24. Start with the ones: 8 minus 4 equals 4.
Now the tens: 6 tens minus 2 tens equals 4 tens. Put it together: 44! So 68 minus 24 equals 44.
Great work! Subtracting by place value is so powerful. Let's practice some more!
Now for the trickier kind — subtracting with regrouping! Sometimes we need to borrow from the tens place. Let's see how it works.
Let's try 52 minus 37. Look at the ones: we have 2 and need to take away 7. We can't do that, so we borrow from the tens!
Borrow 1 ten from the 5, making it 4. Now the ones become 12. 12 minus 7 equals 5. Then 4 tens minus 3 tens equals 1 ten. So the answer is 15!
You handled regrouping like a champ! That was the hard part — and you got it. Practice makes perfect!

lessons.title: One- and Two-Step Subtraction Word Problems

steps.text: Now let's use subtraction to solve stories! Reading carefully is a big part of math. Let's find out what to subtract.
Mia has 73 stickers. She gives 28 stickers to her friend Leo. How many stickers does Mia have left? We subtract: 73 minus 28.
Regroup: borrow from the 7. Ones: 13 minus 8 equals 5. Tens: 6 minus 2 equals 4. So Mia has 45 stickers left!
You solved a real story problem! That's amazing math. Let's practice reading and solving more word problems!
Third grade subtraction — let's go big! We'll subtract numbers in the hundreds using place value. This is where your brain really gets to shine.
Let's try 700 minus 300. Think of it as 7 hundreds minus 3 hundreds. 7 minus 3 equals 4 hundreds.
700 minus 300 equals 400. Just like subtracting ones, but with hundreds! You're doing brilliantly.
Fantastic! Place value makes subtracting hundreds so clear. Let's practice big number subtraction!
Now let's subtract numbers up to 1000 with multiple place values. You've been practicing place value — now let's put it all together!
Let's subtract 583 minus 247. Ones: 3 minus 7 — we need to regroup! Borrow from the tens. Now 13 minus 7 equals 6.
Tens: 7 minus 4 equals 3 (remember we borrowed one ten). Hundreds: 5 minus 2 equals 3. So 583 minus 247 equals 336!
Outstanding work! Subtracting within 1000 is a big skill and you nailed it. Time to practice!
Two-step problems with subtraction! These need two math steps to solve. Read carefully and we'll work through it together.
Zara has 850 points in a game. She loses 200 points, then loses 150 more. How many points does she have now?
Step one: 850 minus 200 equals 650. Step two: 650 minus 150 equals 500. Zara has 500 points!
Then 650 - 150 = 500. Two steps, one amazing answer! You're an incredible math thinker!
Story problems are math hiding inside little stories! Let's listen to the story and figure out what we need to add.
Here's our story: Mia found 4 seashells on the beach. Then she found 3 more seashells. How many seashells does she have in all?
The words 'in all' tell us to add! 4 plus 3 equals 7. Mia has 7 seashells. You found the math in the story!
You're a fantastic story-problem solver! Let's practice reading stories and finding the addition.
Now let's find subtraction hiding in stories! Listen for words that mean taking away.
Here's the story: Remy the Fox had 8 blueberries. He ate 3 blueberries for a snack. How many blueberries does he have left?
The word 'left' tells us to subtract! 8 minus 3 equals 5. Remy has 5 blueberries. You spotted the subtraction!
Brilliant detective work! You found the math hiding in the story. Let's practice more taking-away stories!
Sometimes a story asks us to compare — to find out how many more or how many fewer. Let's learn how to solve comparing stories!
Leo has 9 crayons. Zara has 6 crayons. How many more crayons does Leo have than Zara?
To compare, we subtract the smaller number from the bigger one. 9 minus 6 equals 3. Leo has 3 more crayons than Zara!
You compared two amounts and found the difference. That's such smart math thinking! Let's practice!
Second-grade story problems have bigger numbers! The strategy is the same — read the story, find the key words, and decide whether to add or subtract.
Mia collects 46 trading cards. Her friend gives her 27 more. How many cards does Mia have in all?
We add because Mia is getting more cards. 6 plus 7 equals 13, carry the 1. 4 plus 2 plus 1 equals 7 tens. Mia has 73 cards!
Reading carefully and finding the operation — that's real math problem-solving! Let's do more!
Ready for two-step word problems? These stories need TWO math operations to solve. Take it one step at a time!
Leo has 35 stamps. He buys 24 more stamps at the shop. Then he gives 18 stamps to his sister. How many stamps does Leo have now?
Step one: 35 plus 24 equals 59. Now step two: 59 minus 18 equals 41. Leo has 41 stamps. Two steps — you solved it!
Then 59 - 18 = 41. Two steps, one great answer! You're a two-step math hero!
Let's compare bigger numbers in stories! These problems ask 'how many more' or 'how many fewer' — and we use subtraction to find the answer.
Zara read 63 pages of her book. Remy read 48 pages of his book. How many more pages did Zara read than Remy?
We subtract to compare: 63 minus 48. Regroup: 13 minus 8 equals 5. 5 tens minus 4 tens equals 1 ten. Zara read 15 more pages!
Incredible comparing skills! You used subtraction to find the difference. Let's practice some more comparing stories!
Third-grade word problems use really big numbers! Today's problems need two addition steps. Read carefully, and we'll work through it together.
A park has 325 oak trees and 248 maple trees. Park rangers plant 112 more oak trees. How many trees are in the park now?
Step one: 325 plus 248 equals 573. Step two: 573 plus 112 equals 685. The park has 685 trees!
Then 573 + 112 = 685. Two-step addition with big numbers — you're a superstar problem solver!
Two-step problems with subtraction! These stories need two steps — and they might both be subtraction, or one add and one subtract.
A school has 920 students. On a field trip day, 135 students go to the science museum and 245 students go to the art gallery. How many students stay at school?
Step one: 920 minus 135 equals 785. Step two: 785 minus 245 equals 540. 540 students stay at school!
Then 785 - 245 = 540. You solved a two-step subtraction story — that's truly impressive math!
The ultimate challenge — mixed two-step problems! Some steps add and some subtract. Read every word carefully and plan your steps.
A school library has 450 books. They receive 230 new books as a donation. Then 180 books are borrowed by students. How many books are on the shelves now?
Step one (add): 450 plus 230 equals 680. Step two (subtract): 680 minus 180 equals 500. There are 500 books on the shelves!
Then 680 - 180 = 500. You solved a mixed two-step problem! You are an incredible mathematician and you should be so proud!

problems.question: There are 3 rows of books with 5 books in each row. How many books are there in all?
There are 4 rows of tiles with 4 tiles in each row. What is 4 + 4 + 4 + 4?
A farm has 215 chickens. They get 130 more in spring and 95 more in summer. How many chickens are there now?
Mia collects 348 rocks. She finds 125 more at the beach and 67 more in the park. How many rocks does she have in all?
A school raises 412 dollars on day one and 388 dollars on day two. How much money did they raise in total?
Leo had 85 marbles. He gave 37 marbles to his friend. How many marbles does Leo have left?
Zara has 91 stickers. She uses 46 of them in her scrapbook. How many stickers does Zara have left?
There are 78 flowers in a garden. 29 flowers are picked for a bouquet. How many flowers are still in the garden?
A shelf has 65 books. A student checks out 18 books. How many books are on the shelf now?
Mia has 82 beads. She uses 35 beads to make a bracelet. How many beads does she have left?
Leo has 750 building blocks. He uses 230 to build a castle and 180 more to build a bridge. How many blocks does he have left?
A bookstore has 900 books. They sell 345 books on Saturday and 215 books on Sunday. How many books are left?
Mia has 685 stickers. She gives away 140 to her class and 245 to her family. How many stickers does Mia have now?
Mia has 3 apples. She picks 4 more apples from the tree. How many apples does she have now?
Remy sees 2 butterflies in the garden. Then 5 more butterflies fly in. How many butterflies are there now?
Leo has 4 toy cars. His dad gives him 3 more toy cars. How many toy cars does Leo have in all?
Zara has 5 books on her shelf. She gets 4 more books as a gift. How many books does she have in all?
There are 6 birds on a tree. Then 3 more birds land on the tree. How many birds are on the tree now?
Remy had 8 blueberries. He ate 3 blueberries for a snack. How many blueberries does he have left?
Mia had 9 balloons. The wind blew 4 balloons away. How many balloons does she have left?
Leo had 10 grapes. He shared 6 grapes with Zara. How many grapes does Leo have now?
There were 7 fish in a bowl. 2 fish were moved to a bigger tank. How many fish are still in the bowl?
Zara had 8 stickers. She gave 5 stickers to her friend. How many stickers does Zara have now?
Leo has 9 crayons. Zara has 6 crayons. How many more crayons does Leo have than Zara?
Mia has 7 books. Remy has 4 books. How many more books does Mia have than Remy?
There are 8 red blocks and 5 blue blocks. How many more red blocks are there than blue blocks?
Zara collected 10 rocks and Remy collected 7 rocks. How many more rocks did Zara collect than Remy?
Leo has 9 stickers. Mia has 4 stickers. How many fewer stickers does Mia have than Leo?
Mia collects 46 trading cards. Her friend gives her 27 more. How many cards does Mia have in all?
Leo has 38 building blocks. He gets 25 more blocks for his birthday. How many blocks does he have now?
A basket has 54 oranges. 29 more oranges are added to the basket. How many oranges are in the basket now?
Zara read 42 pages on Monday and 35 pages on Tuesday. How many pages did she read in all?
Remy has 67 stickers. He buys 18 more stickers at the shop. How many stickers does Remy have now?
Leo has 35 stamps. He buys 24 more stamps at the shop. Then he gives 18 stamps to his sister. How many stamps does Leo have now?
Mia has 48 beads. She finds 23 more beads and then uses 31 beads to make a necklace. How many beads does Mia have left?
A class has 56 pencils. They get 34 more pencils. Then they use 27 pencils for a project. How many pencils are left?
Zara has 72 coins. She earns 18 more coins. Then she spends 35 coins at the fair. How many coins does Zara have now?
Remy collected 41 rocks. He found 29 more rocks. Then he gave 15 rocks to Leo. How many rocks does Remy have now?
Zara read 63 pages of her book. Remy read 48 pages of his book. How many more pages did Zara read than Remy?
Mia has 82 stickers. Leo has 57 stickers. How many more stickers does Mia have than Leo?
A jar has 91 marbles. Another jar has 68 marbles. How many more marbles are in the first jar?
Remy ran 74 laps. Zara ran 59 laps. How many fewer laps did Zara run than Remy?
Leo collected 85 leaves. Mia collected 67 leaves. How many more leaves did Leo collect than Mia?
A park has 325 oak trees and 248 maple trees. Park rangers plant 112 more oak trees. How many trees are in the park now?
Mia's school collected 416 cans of food in October and 283 more cans in November. Then they collected 151 more cans in December. How many cans did they collect in all?
Remy read 234 pages in July and 318 pages in August. How many pages did Remy read over the two months?
A garden center has 145 rose plants and 267 tulip plants. They get a delivery of 88 more rose plants. How many plants are there now?
Leo scored 275 points in the first round of a game and 348 points in the second round. What is his total score?
A school has 920 students. On a field trip day, 135 students go to the science museum and 245 students go to the art gallery. How many students stay at school?
Zara's town has 875 library books. Some kids check out 234 books in the morning and 148 books in the afternoon. How many books are left on the shelves?
A bakery bakes 756 cookies. They sell 312 cookies at the morning sale and 188 cookies in the afternoon. How many cookies are left?
Mia's class collected 643 cans. They donate 215 cans to one shelter and 178 cans to another shelter. How many cans does the class still have?
Remy has 810 building blocks. He uses 365 blocks to build a tower and 245 blocks to build a wall. How many blocks does he have left?
A school library has 450 books. They receive 230 new books as a donation. Then 180 books are borrowed by students. How many books are on the shelves now?
Leo's town planted 567 trees in spring. A storm knocked down 89 trees. Then they planted 122 more trees. How many trees are there now?
Mia had 720 beads. She used 345 beads to make jewelry and then bought 125 more beads. How many beads does she have now?
Zara's team earned 485 points. They earned 235 more points in round two. Then they lost 120 points for a wrong answer. How many points does the team have now?
Remy collected 634 stamps. He gave away 156 stamps and then received 72 stamps as a gift. How many stamps does Remy have now?
